titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Samuel P. O'Reilly Papers AIA.098
titlestmt.author: Shannon O'Neill, Rachel Searcy

publicationstmt.publisher: Tamiment Library and Robert F. Wagner Labor Archives

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2026-02-26 21:17:23 UTC.

repository.corpname: Tamiment Library and Robert F. Wagner Labor Archives

did.abstract: Samuel (Sam) P. O'Reilly (1896-1988) was a founding member of the Irish Institute, a delegate of the United Irish Counties Association (UICA), and a president of the Dublin Society of New York. Samuel joined the Irish Volunteers (Óglaigh na hÉireann) during their first meeting in November 1913, and acted as a gun runner from Howth. During the 1916 Easter Rising, he was active at Cabra, Phibsboro, Upper O'Connell Street, and the General Post Office. After surrendering, O'Reilly was imprisoned at Knutsford Prison and the Frangoch internment camp until the 1917 amnesty granted to all remaining prisoners of the Easter Rising, after which he continued his activities with the Dublin Brigade of the Irish Republican Army until the 1921 truce. In 1922, after travelling in the service of the Irish Republican Brotherhood, Samuel O'Reilly moved to New York City. The Samuel P. O'Reilly Papers (dated 1916-1988, bulk 1948-1988) consist of materials document O'Reilly's participation in the 1916 Easter Uprising in Ireland, as well as his attempts to document his observances of 20th century Irish history. Materials include newspaper clippings, a booklet commemorating the 50th anniversary of the Easter Rising, a Bible dated from when his father and brother were incarcerated at H.M. Prison Wandsworth, and writings by O'Reilly, which includes poetry and memories of Irish Republican Army (IRA) activities from 1918-1920. The collection also includes photographs of the O'Reilly family, a sign requesting donations to the IRA, and activities of the Dublin Society of New York.
did.physloc: Materials are stored offsite and advance notice is required for use. Please request materials at least two business days prior to your research visit to coordinate access.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Samuel P. O'Reilly Papers (dated 1916-1988, bulk 1948-1988) consist of materials document O'Reilly's participation in the 1916 Easter Rising in Ireland, as well as his attempts to document his observances of 20th century Irish history. Materials include newspaper clippings, a booklet commemorating the 50th anniversary of the Easter Rising, a Bible dated from when his father and brother were incarcerated at H.M. Prison Wandsworth, and writings by O'Reilly, which includes poetry and memories of Irish Republican Army (IRA) activities from 1918-1920. The collection also includes photographs of the O'Reilly family, a sign requesting donations to the IRA, and activities of the Dublin Society of New York.

bioghist.head: Biographical Note
bioghist.p: Samuel (Sam) P. O'Reilly (1896-1988) was a founding member of the Irish Institute, a delegate of the United Irish Counties Association (UICA), and a president of the Dublin Society of New York. O'Reilly was born in Dublin to John Kevin (J.K.) O'Reilly, who wrote the ballad "Wrap the Green Flag 'Round Me Boys" and hosted meetings for nationalists and the Celtic Literary Society. Samuel joined the Irish Volunteers (Óglaigh na hÉireann) during their first meeting in November 1913, and acted as a gun runner from Howth. During the 1916 Easter Rising, he was active at Cabra, Phibsboro, Upper O'Connell Street, and the General Post Office (GPO). While the GPO was on fire, Patrick Pearse commanded O'Reilly to search the rear of the building to ensure that no soldiers would be left behind. Pearse and O'Reilly were the last two men to leave the building. After surrendering, O'Reilly was imprisoned at Knutsford Prison and the Frangoch internment camp, which was primarily used to incarcerate German prisoners of war. He was incarcerated until the 1917 amnesty granted to all remaining prisoners of the Easter Rising, after which he continued his activities with the Dublin Brigade of the Irish Republican Army until the 1921 truce. He participated in the 1920 Mountjoy Hunger Strike. O'Reilly's father J.K. and his brothers Desmond, Kevin, Donal, and Tommy all fought in the Easter Rising, with Donal (age 12) and Tommy (age 14) having been involved by way of Fianna Éireann. After the Rising, J.K. and Kevin were deported and incarcerated in Wandsworth Prison. Donal joined the International Brigades in 1936 to fight for the Popular Front during the Spanish Civil War. In 1922, after travelling in the service of the Irish Republican Brotherhood, Samuel O'Reilly moved to New York City. He worked as an engineer at Columbia University, and was active in many Irish and Irish American organizations until his retirement in 1970.
"An Old Soldier Dies." Irish Echo, July 30, 1988.
Mike McCormack, "Memories of the Easter Rising." Irish Echo, undated.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: This collection is protected by copyright and/or related rights. You are free to use materials in the collection in any way that is permitted by the copyright and related rights legislation that applies to your use. For other uses you need to obtain permission from the rights-holder(s).

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Transferred by Marion Casey in December 2025; the accession number associated with this transfer is 2026.028.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: At an unknown date, Geraldine O'Reilly Johnson, Samuel P. O'Reilly's daughter, gifted the collection to Marion Casey.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Identification of item, date; Samuel P. O'Reilly Papers; AIA 098; box number; folder number or item identifier; Tamiment Library/Robert F. Wagner Labor Archives, New York University.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: At the time of accessioning, materials were rehoused in archival boxes and folders, described on the collection-level, arranged alphabetically, and inventoried at the folder-level.

did.unittitle: 50th Easter Week Commemoration Banquet Booklet (Dublin Society of New York)
